old_claim.note: Advanced Metallurgy (Medieval) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Hardened steel or iron striking surfaces, springs, barrels, and fitted lock components are enabling material capabilities for reliable flintlock ignition.

source_shape.source_locator: The Flintlock Mechanism section explains the flint-and-steel spark process and identifies the frizzen as steel, with mainspring and frizzen spring components in the operating lock.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes the metal striking surface and spring components that make flintlock ignition work.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That component evidence supports the metallurgy prerequisite because reliable frizzen, spring, barrel, and lock parts depend on ferrous material capability, not only firearm concept lineage.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: advanced_metallurgy_medieval remains an enabling edge, now tied to specific steel and spring components.
would_reject_if: A stronger source showed early true flintlocks used no steel or ferrous spring components.
The advanced_metallurgy_medieval node were narrowed away from ferrous steelworking and heat-treated components.
The edge were interpreted as a unique invention dependency rather than an enabling material capability.
short_reason: Steel striking and spring components make metallurgy an enabling capability.
